aliases: Seared Tuna with Rocket and Mozzarella

english: Seared Tuna with Rocket and Mozzarella

food_subgroup: Seared tuna dish with salad accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the prepared Italian tagliata di tonno dish. FOODON_03302051 (yellowfin tuna food product) is used as the closest parent representing the primary ingredient.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (yellowfin tuna, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for the composite tagliata di tonno preparation with vegetables, olives, mozzarella, and olive oil.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this composite Italian tuna dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a seared yellowfin tuna loin portion with cherry tomatoes, black olives, rocket leaves, mozzarella, red onion, and a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: Yellowfin tuna, cooked, dry heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15264) combined with component estimation for olive oil, mozzarella, rocket, cherry tomatoes, black olives, and red onion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite tagliata di tonno dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 55% seared yellowfin tuna loin (USDA SR Legacy #15264), 10% mozzarella (USDA SR Legacy #01028), 8% extra virgin olive oil (USDA SR Legacy #04053), 10% cherry tomatoes (USDA SR Legacy #11529), 5% black olives (USDA SR Legacy #09193), 5% rocket/arugula (USDA SR Legacy #11959), 4% red onion (USDA SR Legacy #11282), 3% mixed Mediterranean herbs. All component values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Weighted blend calculated per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood and salad dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tagliata di tonno. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous low-carbohydrate mixed seafood and salad dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is dominated by protein (seared tuna, mozzarella) and fat (olive oil), with minimal carbohydrate from cherry tomatoes, olives, and rocket. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (32 × 2.8 × 3.5) / 100 = 3.1 g. This is consistent with low-GI, low-GL classification for high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. Reference: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from black olives, mozzarella, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium contribution (~210 mg per 100 g, approximately 735 mg per serving) from olives, mozzarella, and added seasoning; requesting reduced olives or no added salt can lower sodium intake meaningfully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load of approximately 3.1 per serving, tagliata di tonno is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion; the high protein content from tuna and mozzarella further supports glycaemic stability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from yellowfin tuna and monounsaturated fats from extra virgin olive oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yellowfin tuna provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) and the extra virgin olive oil contributes monounsaturated fats consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern; the relatively low saturated fat content (~2.1 g per 100 g) makes this dish appropriate for cardiovascular disease management when sodium from olives and cheese is moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tagliata di Tonno is a classic Italian dish featuring a seared, black pepper-crusted yellowfin tuna loin that is sliced and served over a bed of fresh rocket leaves (arugula), accompanied by cherry tomatoes, black olives, thinly sliced red onion, mozzarella cheese, and a generous drizzle of organic extra virgin olive oil, finished with Mediterranean herbs. The name 'tagliata' derives from the Italian verb 'tagliare' (to cut or slice), reflecting the characteristic presentation of the protein sliced after searing. The tuna loin is typically seared at high heat to achieve a caramelised, peppery crust while retaining a rare-to-medium-rare pink centre, yielding a tender, meaty texture with a clean oceanic flavour balanced by the peppery bitterness of rocket, the acidity of cherry tomatoes, the brininess of olives, and the creamy richness of mozzarella. Nutritionally, this dish is high in lean protein (~16.8 g per 100 g), rich in omega-3 fatty acids from yellowfin tuna, and provides monounsaturated fats from extra virgin olive oil consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern. Carbohydrate content is very low (~2.8 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary plans. The dish carries a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~32) and a low glycemic load per serving (~3.1). Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease, though sodium from olives and mozzarella warrants attention for those with hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk, typically as a main course priced at approximately SGD 28.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite tagliata di tonno dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for each ingredient. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable low-carbohydrate, high-protein seafood and salad dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' — the dish is a seared tuna preparation and is not a grain or starch dish. 2. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite Italian dish. All components sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as yellowfin tuna and extra virgin olive oil contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fats in mozzarella are below detection threshold. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~32) based on low-carbohydrate mixed seafood and salad dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tagliata di tonno was found in approved sources. 5. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this Italian dish; the cuisine is Italian and the mandatory multilingual rules apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes only. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not -1 sentinel values. 7. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains mozzarella and the halal certification of the restaurant is not confirmed from approved sources. 8. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a restaurant main-course portion; no official serving size was found in approved sources.
